title: Bruschetta al Pomodoro
description: Classic Tuscan starter featuring toasted bread topped with fresh tomatoes, basil, and garlic. A simple yet flavourful dish that highlights the quality of summer produce.

steps.instruction: Slice the ciabatta into 1 cm thick slices and toast in the oven at 180°C for 5 minutes until golden.
Dice the tomatoes and place in a bowl. Finely chop the basil and mince the garlic. Add both to the tomatoes along with olive oil, salt, and black pepper. Mix gently.
Spoon the tomato mixture onto the toasted bread slices and serve immediately.

wine_pairing_notes: A light Pinot Grigio complements the fresh flavours.
tips: Use the ripest tomatoes for the best flavour
Toast the bread just before serving to keep it crisp

title: Risotto ai Funghi
description: Creamy risotto infused with wild mushrooms and Parmesan cheese. A comforting dish that brings the earthy flavours of autumn to the table.

steps.instruction: Clean the mushrooms and slice them. Finely chop the shallots and mince the garlic.
Heat olive oil and half the butter in a large pan. Sauté the shallots and garlic for 3 minutes until soft.
Add the mushrooms and cook for 5 minutes until they release their moisture and start to brown.
Add the rice and stir for 2 minutes to toast lightly. Pour in the wine and let it evaporate completely.
Start adding the hot stock one ladle at a time, stirring frequently. Wait until each addition is absorbed before adding the next. This should take about 20 minutes.
When the rice is al dente, stir in the remaining butter and Parmesan. Adjust seasoning with salt and pepper. Serve immediately.

wine_pairing_notes: A full-bodied white wine like Gavi di Gavi pairs beautifully.
tips: Stir constantly to release the starch and create a creamy texture
Use a good quality stock for the best flavour

title: Saltimbocca alla Romana
description: A classic Roman dish of veal cutlets topped with prosciutto and sage, cooked in white wine butter sauce. Quick to prepare but packed with flavour.

steps.instruction: Lay the veal cutlets between cling film and pound them thin with a meat mallet.
Place a slice of prosciutto and 2 sage leaves on each cutlet. Roll up and secure with a toothpick.
Heat olive oil and half the butter in a frying pan. Brown the saltimbocca on all sides for 2-3 minutes total. Remove from pan and set aside.
Add the remaining butter to the pan. Pour in the wine and let it reduce by half. Return the saltimbocca to the pan and cook for another minute.
Serve immediately with a drizzle of the sauce.

wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied red like Montepulciano d'Abruzzo is ideal.
tips: Use very thin veal cutlets for best results
Don't overcook the veal to keep it tender

title: Pasta alla Norma
description: A beloved Sicilian pasta dish featuring fried aubergines, ricotta salata, and a simple tomato sauce. The combination of textures and flavours is simply divine.

steps.instruction: Dice the aubergines and toss with 10g salt. Let sit for 10 minutes then pat dry with kitchen paper.
Fry the aubergines in 60ml olive oil in batches until golden brown. Drain on kitchen paper and sprinkle with salt.
Blanch the tomatoes in boiling water for 30 seconds, then peel and chop. Crush the garlic.
Heat remaining oil in a pan. Sauté the garlic for 1 minute then add the tomatoes and red pepper flakes. Simmer for 10 minutes until thickened.
Cook the pasta in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 100ml pasta water then drain.
Toss the pasta with the tomato sauce, adding pasta water as needed. Stir in the aubergines and basil. Serve with grated ricotta salata on top.

wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Sicilian red like Nero d'Avola pairs perfectly.
tips: Salt the aubergines to remove bitterness and improve texture
Fry the aubergines in batches to avoid overcrowding the pan

title: Tiramisu Classico
description: The iconic Italian dessert of coffee-soaked ladyfingers layered with mascarpone cream and dusted with cocoa. A perfect end to any meal.

steps.instruction: Brew the coffee and let it cool. Whisk the egg yolks with 60g sugar until pale and thick. Add the mascarpone and mix until smooth.
Beat the egg whites with the remaining sugar until stiff peaks form. Fold gently into the mascarpone mixture.
Quickly dip each ladyfinger in the coffee (do not soak) and arrange a layer in a dish. Sprinkle with amaretto if using.
Spread half the mascarpone cream over the ladyfingers. Repeat with another layer of dipped ladyfingers and cream.
Chill in the fridge for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight. Before serving, dust generously with cocoa powder and grate dark chocolate on top.

wine_pairing_notes: A sweet Vin Santo or espresso are traditional pairings.
tips: Use room temperature eggs for easier whisking
Chill for as long as possible for best texture

title: Caprese Salad
description: A classic Italian starter highlighting the freshness of summer produce. Simple yet bursting with flavour, it's a celebration of ripe tomatoes, creamy mozzarella, and fragrant basil.

steps.instruction: Slice the tomatoes and mozzarella into 1cm thick rounds. Arrange them alternately on a platter, slightly overlapping.
Tuck fresh basil leaves between the tomato and mozzarella slices.
Drizzle with olive oil and balsamic vinegar. Sprinkle with sea salt and black pepper.

wine_pairing_notes: A light Pinot Grigio complements the fresh flavours.
tips: Use the ripest tomatoes for the best taste. Avoid overhandling the ingredients.

title: Spaghetti alla Carbonara
description: Rome's iconic pasta dish made with eggs, Pecorino Romano, guanciale, and black pepper. Rich, creamy, and deeply flavourful without any cream.

steps.instruction: Cook spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 1 cup of pasta water before draining.
In a pan, fry diced guanciale on medium heat until crispy. Remove from heat and set aside.
In a bowl, whisk eggs with grated Pecorino Romano and black pepper.
Add drained spaghetti to the pan with guanciale. Remove from heat, then quickly stir in the egg mixture, adding reserved pasta water as needed to create a creamy sauce.

wine_pairing_notes: A bold red like Montepulciano d'Abruzzo pairs excellently.
tips: Use room temperature eggs to prevent scrambling. Do not let the egg mixture sit in the pan too long after mixing.

title: Risotto ai Funghi
description: A luxurious risotto made with wild mushrooms, Arborio rice, white wine, and Parmesan. Creamy, earthy, and deeply satisfying, perfect for a cosy dinner.

steps.instruction: Finely chop the mushrooms. Finely dice the shallots and mince the garlic.
In a wide pan, heat olive oil and 10g butter over medium heat. Sauté shallots and garlic until soft.
Add chopped mushrooms and thyme, cooking until they release their moisture and turn golden. Season with salt and pepper.
Add Arborio rice, stirring to coat with oil and mushrooms. Pour in white wine and cook until absorbed.
Add hot vegetable stock one ladle at a time, stirring constantly until each addition is absorbed before adding the next (approx. 18-20 minutes).
Remove from heat, stir in remaining butter and grated Parmesan. Season to taste and serve immediately.

wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied white like Verdicchio pairs beautifully.
tips: Stir frequently for a creamy texture. Use homemade stock for deeper flavour. Add stock gradually to prevent a soupy risotto.

title: Melanzane alla Parmigiana
description: A baked dish of fried aubergine layered with tomato sauce, mozzarella, and Parmesan. Hearty, cheesy, and full of Mediterranean flavours.

steps.instruction: Slice aubergines into 1cm thick rounds. Sprinkle with salt and let sit for 20 minutes to remove bitterness. Pat dry.
Beat eggs in a bowl. Place flour in another bowl. Dip aubergine slices in flour, then egg.
Heat olive oil in a deep pan. Fry aubergine slices in batches until golden. Drain on kitchen paper.
In a saucepan, heat butter and sauté garlic. Add passata, salt, and pepper. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Layer half the aubergine slices in a baking dish. Top with half the tomato sauce, mozzarella, Parmesan, and basil. Repeat layers.
Bake at 180°C for 25 minutes until golden and bubbling. Let rest for 10 minutes before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: A full-bodied red like Nero d'Avola complements the richness.
tips: Salt the aubergines well to remove bitterness. Fry in batches to avoid overcrowding the pan. Use fresh basil for the best flavour.

title: Tiramisu
description: Italy's most famous dessert, featuring layers of coffee-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream. Light, creamy, and utterly indulgent.

steps.instruction: Prepare coffee and let it cool. If using, mix brandy into coffee.
Separate egg whites from yolks. Whisk yolks with 60g sugar until pale and thick. Add mascarpone and whisk until smooth.
Beat egg whites with remaining sugar until stiff peaks form. Gently fold into mascarpone mixture.
Dip ladyfingers briefly in coffee and arrange a layer in a dish. Cover with a layer of mascarpone cream.
Repeat layers. Dust top with cocoa powder. Chill for at least 4 hours before serving. Shave dark chocolate on top if desired.

wine_pairing_notes: A sweet wine like Vin Santo pairs well.
tips: Use fresh eggs for the best texture. Chill the tiramisu well before serving. Avoid soaking the ladyfingers too long to prevent a soggy texture.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: A classic Roman dish with a simple yet flavorful combination of garlic, olive oil, and chili. Quick to prepare and perfect for a light meal.

steps.instruction: Cook the spaghetti in a large pot of boiling salted water until al dente. Reserve 1 cup of pasta water before draining.
In a large pan, heat the olive oil over low heat. Add the minced garlic and chili flakes, sautéing until the garlic is golden but not browned.
Add the drained spaghetti to the pan and toss to coat in the garlic oil. Add a splash of reserved pasta water to emulsify the sauce.
Season with salt to taste, stir in the chopped parsley, and serve immediately.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a light Pinot Grigio to complement the garlic and chili flavors.
tips: Use the best quality olive oil you can afford for the best flavor.
Don't overcook the garlic to avoid bitterness.

title: Risotto ai Funghi
description: A creamy risotto featuring fresh wild mushrooms and Parmesan cheese. Rich and satisfying, perfect for a special occasion.

steps.instruction: Finely chop the onion, garlic, and mushrooms. Heat olive oil and half the butter in a large pan over medium heat. Add onion and garlic, sauté until softened.
Add the mushrooms and rosemary, cooking until the mushrooms release their moisture and it evaporates. Season with salt and pepper.
Add the risotto rice to the pan, stirring to coat the grains with oil. Pour in the white wine and cook until fully absorbed.
Gradually add the hot vegetable stock, one ladle at a time, stirring frequently and allowing the liquid to absorb before adding more. This should take about 16-18 minutes.
Once the rice is creamy and al dente, remove from heat. Stir in the remaining butter and Parmesan cheese. Adjust seasoning if needed. Cover and let rest for 2 minutes before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a full-bodied white wine such as a Chardonnay to match the creamy texture and umami flavors.
tips: Use a good quality vegetable stock for the best flavor.
Stir frequently but avoid over-stirring to achieve the perfect creamy texture.

title: Melanzane alla Parmigiana
description: Layers of fried aubergine, tomato sauce, and melted cheese. A comforting dish that brings together the flavors of Southern Italy.

steps.instruction: Slice the aubergines into 1 cm thick rounds. Sprinkle with salt and let sit for 20 minutes to draw out excess moisture. Pat dry with paper towels.
Heat olive oil in a large pan over medium heat. Fry the aubergine slices in batches until golden brown on both sides, about 3-4 minutes per side. Drain on paper towels.
In a separate pan, sauté finely chopped onion and garlic in a little olive oil until softened. Add the tomato passata, season with salt and pepper, and simmer for 10 minutes. Stir in chopped basil.
Preheat the oven to 190°C (375°F). In a baking dish, layer half the fried aubergine slices, half the tomato sauce, half the mozzarella, and a sprinkle of Parmesan. Repeat the layers.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until the top is golden and bubbling. Let rest for 5 minutes before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a medium-bodied red wine such as a Chianti to complement the tomato and cheese flavors.
tips: Slicing the aubergine thinly ensures even cooking and a better texture.
You can prepare the dish ahead and bake it just before serving for convenience.

title: Pasta alla Norma
description: A Sicilian classic featuring pasta with a rich tomato and aubergine sauce, topped with ricotta salata. Simple yet bursting with flavor.

steps.instruction: Cut the aubergines into 1 cm cubes. Sprinkle with salt and let sit for 15 minutes to draw out moisture. Pat dry with paper towels.
Heat olive oil in a large pan over medium heat. Fry the aubergine cubes in batches until golden brown. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, sauté finely chopped onion and garlic until softened. Add the tomato passata, season with salt and pepper, and cook for 10 minutes.
Add the fried aubergine and chopped basil to the tomato sauce. Cook for another 5 minutes to meld the flavors.
Cook the pasta in a large pot of boiling salted water until al dente. Reserve 1 cup of pasta water before draining.
Toss the drained pasta with the aubergine sauce, adding a splash of reserved pasta water if needed. Serve topped with grated ricotta salata.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a medium-bodied red wine such as a Nero d'Avola to complement the rich tomato and aubergine flavors.
tips: Do not skip salting the aubergine to remove excess moisture, which can make the dish watery.
Use a good quality passata for a richer tomato flavor.

title: Focaccia Genovese
description: A traditional Genoese focaccia with a soft, fluffy interior and a crispy, olive oil-kissed crust. Perfect as an appetizer or snack.

steps.instruction: In a small bowl, dissolve the yeast and sugar in warm water. Let sit for 5 minutes until frothy.
In a large bowl, mix the flour and salt. Make a well in the center and pour in the yeast mixture and olive oil. Stir until a shaggy dough forms.
Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and let rise for 1 hour or until doubled in size.
Preheat the oven to 220°C (425°F). Grease a 20x30 cm baking tray with olive oil. Spread the dough out in the tray, pressing it to the edges. Dimple the dough with your fingertips.
Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle with sea salt flakes and rosemary (if using). Let rise for another 20 minutes.
Bake for 20-25 minutes until golden brown. Serve warm or at room temperature.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a light sparkling wine such as Prosecco to complement the crispy texture and olive oil.
tips: For extra flavor, add a few pitted olives or cherry tomatoes on top before baking.
The dough should be sticky; resist the urge to add too much extra flour.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: A classic Roman pasta dish with a simple garlic and olive oil sauce. Quick and easy to prepare for any weeknight dinner.

steps.instruction: Cook the spaghetti in a large pot of salted boiling water until al dente, according to package instructions.
While the pasta cooks, heat the olive oil in a large pan over low heat. Add the garlic cloves and cook until lightly golden, about 2 minutes.
Add the chilli flakes to the pan and stir for 30 seconds. Remove from heat and set aside.
Drain the spaghetti, reserving 1 cup of pasta water. Add the spaghetti to the pan with the garlic oil and toss to coat.
If needed, add a splash of pasta water to loosen the sauce. Season with salt to taste.
Serve immediately, garnished with chopped parsley and grated Parmesan cheese if desired.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a crisp white wine such as Frascati Superiore from Lazio.
tips: Use good quality olive oil for the best flavor.
For a vegan version, omit the Parmesan cheese or use a dairy-free alternative.

title: Eggplant Parmigiana
description: A rich and hearty layered dish featuring fried aubergine, tomato sauce, and melted cheese. Perfect for a festive occasion or a weekend meal.

steps.instruction: Slice the aubergines into 1/2 cm thick rounds. Salt them and let them sit for 30 minutes to remove bitterness. Pat dry with paper towels.
Prepare three bowls: one with flour, one with beaten eggs, and one with breadcrumbs mixed with salt and pepper.
Dredge each aubergine slice in flour, dip in egg, and coat with breadcrumbs. Set aside.
Heat olive oil in a large pan over medium heat. Fry the breaded aubergine slices in batches until golden brown, about 2-3 minutes per side. Drain on paper towels.
For the tomato sauce, heat olive oil in a saucepan. Add garlic and sauté until fragrant. Add tomatoes, basil, salt, and pepper. Simmer for 20 minutes.
In a baking dish, spread a layer of tomato sauce. Top with a layer of fried aubergine slices. Add slices of Mozzarella and sprinkle with Parmesan. Repeat layers, ending with sauce and cheese on top.
Bake in a preheated oven at 180°C for 25-30 minutes, until the top is golden and bubbly.
Let it rest for 10 minutes before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a medium-bodied red wine such as Chianti Classico.
tips: Use young aubergines for the best texture.
For a lighter version, bake the aubergine slices instead of frying them.

title: Risotto alla Milanese
description: A creamy saffron-infused risotto from Milan, traditionally served with ossobuco. A luxurious dish perfect for a special dinner.

steps.instruction: In a large pan, melt half the butter over low heat. Add the finely chopped onion and sauté until translucent, about 5 minutes.
Add the rice to the pan and toast for 2 minutes, stirring constantly to prevent sticking.
Pour in the white wine and stir until it has evaporated completely.

Add a ladleful of hot chicken stock to the rice and stir constantly until the liquid has been absorbed. Repeat this process, adding stock gradually, for about 18-20 minutes until the rice is creamy and al dente.
Once the rice is cooked, stir in the remaining butter, Parmesan cheese, and saffron (dissolved in a little warm stock). Season with salt and pepper to taste.
Cover and let it rest for 2 minutes before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a full-bodied white wine such as Gavi di Gavi or a light red like Valpolicella.
tips: Use a good quality saffron for the best flavor and color.
Stir the risotto constantly to release the starch and achieve a creamy texture.

title: Panzanella Salad
description: A rustic bread salad from Tuscany, perfect for summer. Uses stale bread and fresh vegetables for a refreshing dish.

steps.instruction: Cut the stale bread into large chunks. Soak in water for 1 minute, then squeeze out the excess water and set aside.
Dice the tomatoes, courgette, red onion, and cucumber into bite-sized pieces. Place in a large bowl.
Add the soaked bread to the vegetables. Drizzle with olive oil and red wine vinegar. Toss gently to combine.
Season with salt and pepper to taste. Add the basil leaves, torn into pieces.
Let the salad sit for at least 30 minutes before serving to allow the flavors to meld together.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a light red wine such as Chianti or a crisp white like Vernaccia di San Gimignano.
tips: Use the best quality olive oil you can afford for the best flavor.
For a gluten-free version, substitute the bread with gluten-free bread.

title: Tiramisu
description: A classic Italian dessert featuring layers of coffee-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream. A crowd-pleaser for any occasion.

steps.instruction: Brew the strong coffee and let it cool. If using, mix in the dessert wine.
Separate the egg whites from the yolks. Beat the egg whites until stiff peaks form.
In a separate bowl, beat the egg yolks with the caster sugar until pale and thick. Add the mascarpone cheese and mix until smooth.
Gently fold the beaten egg whites into the mascarpone mixture until just combined.
Quickly dip each ladyfinger into the coffee mixture (do not soak) and arrange a layer in the bottom of a serving dish.
Spread half of the mascarpone cream over the ladyfingers. Repeat with another layer of coffee-dipped ladyfingers and the remaining cream.
Sift cocoa powder evenly over the top. Grate dark chocolate over the cocoa. Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Serve chilled, sliced into portions.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a sweet dessert wine such as Vin Santo or a sparkling wine like Prosecco.
tips: Use fresh, high-quality mascarpone cheese for the best flavor.
For a stronger coffee flavor, add a teaspoon of instant coffee to the brewed coffee.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio

steps.instruction: Cook spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 100ml pasta water, then drain.

title: Pasta al Pesto di Rucola
description: A fresh and peppery pesto made with rocket (arugula) instead of basil, tossed with pasta. Quick and vibrant, perfect for summer.

steps.instruction: Cook pasta in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 100ml pasta water, then drain.
Toast pine nuts in a dry pan for 2 minutes until lightly golden.
Blend rocket, garlic, Parmesan, pine nuts, lemon juice, salt, and pepper in a food processor. Stream in olive oil until smooth.
Toss pasta with pesto, adding reserved pasta water as needed to loosen the sauce.
Serve immediately with extra Parmesan and a drizzle of olive oil.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a crisp Italian white like Vermentino or Pinot Grigio.
tips: For a vegan version, replace Parmesan with nutritional yeast.
Add a handful of cherry tomatoes halved for extra freshness.
Use fresh rocket for the brightest flavor.

title: Pollo alla Cacciatora
description: A rustic Tuscan stew of chicken braised with tomatoes, olives, and herbs. Hearty and comforting, ideal for cooler days.

steps.instruction: Chop onion, carrots, celery, and garlic finely. Heat olive oil in a large pan. Season chicken with salt and pepper, then brown on all sides. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, sauté onion, carrots, celery, and garlic until softened. Add rosemary and black olives, cook 2 minutes.
Pour in white wine, let it reduce by half. Add chopped tomatoes, bring to a simmer.
Return chicken to the pan, cover, and simmer for 30 minutes until chicken is tender and sauce thickens.
Serve hot with crusty bread or polenta to soak up the sauce.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a medium-bodied red like Chianti or Sangiovese.
tips: Use bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs for the best flavor and texture.
For extra richness, add a few anchovies while cooking the vegetables.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: A simple yet flavorful dish that highlights the beauty of minimal ingredients. It's quick to prepare and bursting with garlic and olive oil essence.

steps.instruction: Cook the spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 100ml of pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, heat olive oil in a pan over low heat. Add minced garlic and chilli flakes, sautéing until garlic is golden (about 3 minutes).
Add the drained spaghetti to the pan, tossing well to coat. Add reserved pasta water as needed to emulsify the sauce.
Remove from heat, stir in chopped parsley, and season with salt and black pepper to taste.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a light Italian white wine like Vermentino or Pinot Grigio to complement the garlic and oil.
tips: Use high-quality olive oil for the best flavor.
Don’t overcook the garlic to avoid bitterness.

title: Eggplant Parmigiana
description: A layered, baked delight featuring crispy fried aubergine and rich tomato sauce. This dish is a hearty vegetarian classic.

steps.instruction: Slice the aubergine into 1cm thick rounds. Salt both sides and let sit for 20 minutes to remove bitterness. Pat dry with kitchen paper.
Set up three bowls: one with flour, one with beaten eggs, and one with breadcrumbs. Bread each aubergine slice.
Heat olive oil in a pan over medium heat. Fry aubergine slices until golden on both sides (about 3 minutes per side). Drain on paper towels.
In a saucepan, heat olive oil and sauté garlic until fragrant. Add tomato passata, oregano, salt, and pepper. Simmer for 10 minutes.
In a baking dish, layer aubergine slices with tomato sauce, mozzarella, and Parmesan. Repeat layers, finishing with cheese on top.
Bake in a preheated oven at 190°C for 25 minutes or until bubbly and golden. Garnish with fresh basil before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Italian red like Chianti or Sangiovese pairs beautifully with the rich, cheesy layers.
tips: For extra crispiness, double-fry the aubergine slices.
Let the dish rest for 10 minutes after baking to help it set before serving.

title: Risotto ai Funghi
description: Creamy Arborio rice cooked with wild mushrooms and white wine for an umami-rich dish. This risotto is comforting and perfect for autumn evenings.

steps.instruction: Finely chop shallots and garlic. Clean mushrooms and slice into thin pieces.
Heat olive oil and half the butter in a large pan over medium heat. Sauté shallots and garlic until translucent (3 minutes).
Add mushrooms and thyme, cooking until mushrooms release their moisture and it evaporates (8 minutes).
Add Arborio rice to the pan, stirring to coat the grains with oil. Pour in the white wine and cook until fully absorbed.
Add heated stock one ladle at a time, stirring frequently until the liquid is absorbed before adding more (about 20 minutes total).
Once the rice is creamy and al dente, stir in the remaining butter and Parmesan. Season with salt and pepper to taste.

wine_pairing_notes: A full-bodied white wine like Gavi or a light red like Pinot Noir complements the earthy mushrooms.
tips: Use a good-quality Parmesan for the best flavor.
Keep the stock warm in a separate pan to maintain a consistent cooking temperature.

title: Caprese Salad
description: A fresh and vibrant salad with ripe tomatoes, creamy mozzarella, and fragrant basil. Simple yet iconic, perfect for summer.

steps.instruction: Slice tomatoes and mozzarella into 1cm thick rounds. Arrange alternately on a plate.
Tuck fresh basil leaves between the tomato and mozzarella slices.
Drizzle with olive oil and balsamic vinegar. Season with salt and black pepper to taste.

wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Italian white like Vermentino or a light rosé pairs perfectly with the fresh flavors.

Drizzle balsamic vinegar sparingly to avoid overpowering the salad.

title: Tiramisu
description: A classic Italian dessert featuring layers of coffee-soaked sponge and mascarpone cream, dusted with cocoa. Decadent and delightful.

steps.instruction: Brew the coffee and let it cool. If using rum or Marsala, mix into the coffee.
Separate egg whites from yolks. Whisk yolks with 80g sugar until pale and thick (about 5 minutes).
In another bowl, beat egg whites with remaining sugar until stiff peaks form (about 5 minutes).
Gently fold mascarpone into the egg yolk mixture until smooth. Fold in egg whites until fully combined.
Dip each ladyfinger briefly into the coffee (just enough to moisten, not soak) and arrange in a single layer in a dish.
Spread half the mascarpone mixture over the ladyfingers. Repeat with another layer of coffee-dipped ladyfingers and remaining cream.
Grate dark chocolate over the top and dust generously with cocoa powder. Chill for at least 4 hours (or overnight) before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a sweet dessert wine like Vin Santo or a coffee liqueur to enhance the coffee flavors.
tips: Use high-quality coffee for the best flavor.
Let the tiramisu chill for at least 4 hours to allow flavors to meld.

title: Spaghetti alla Carbonara
description: A classic Roman dish with a creamy egg sauce and crispy pancetta. Quick to prepare but requires careful timing.

steps.instruction: Cook the spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve 1 cup of pasta water.
In a bowl, whisk eggs, grated pecorino, and black pepper.
Fry pancetta in a pan until crispy. Remove from heat.
Drain pasta and add to the pan with pancetta. Mix while adding egg mixture and reserved pasta water until creamy.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a bold Italian red like Montepulciano.

Ensure eggs are room temperature to avoid curdling.

description: Creamy polenta topped with rich sausage ragù. A hearty dish from the countryside.

steps.instruction: For the polenta, bring stock to a boil. Slowly whisk in polenta and cook for 20-25 minutes, stirring often.

title: Minestrone alla Genovese
description: A hearty vegetable soup from Genoa, packed with beans, greens, and pasta. A wholesome one-pot dish that’s both nourishing and comforting.

steps.instruction: Soak cannellini beans overnight, then drain and rinse. In a pot, cover beans with water and simmer for 30 minutes until tender.
In a large pot, heat olive oil over medium heat. Sauté finely chopped carrot, celery, and garlic for 5 minutes.
Add diced courgette, green beans, potato, chopped tomatoes, tomato paste, and cabbage. Stir well and cook for 5 minutes.
Pour in 1.5L of water, add the cooked cannellini beans, and bring to a boil. Reduce heat and simmer for 20 minutes. Stir in small pasta and cook for 8-10 minutes.
Season with salt, black pepper, and finely chopped parsley. Serve hot with a sprinkle of Parmigiano-Reggiano.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a light red wine such as Pinot Noir.
tips: Use seasonal vegetables for the best flavor.

title: Osso Buco alla Milanese
description: A traditional Milanese dish of braised veal shanks cooked with vegetables and white wine. Rich and hearty.

steps.instruction: Dice the carrots, celery, and onion. Mince the garlic.
Pat dry the veal shanks and season with salt and pepper. Lightly coat with flour.

Remove the veal shanks and reduce the sauce if needed. Serve the shanks with the sauce and gremolata.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a bold red wine like Barolo or Amarone.
tips: Use a heavy-bottomed pot for even cooking.

title: Tiramisu
description: A beloved Italian dessert made with layers of coffee-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream. Decadent and creamy.

steps.instruction: Brew strong coffee and let it cool. Separate the egg yolks from the whites.
Whisk egg yolks with sugar and vanilla until pale and thick. Add mascarpone and mix until smooth.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a sweet dessert wine like Vin Santo or a sparkling wine like Prosecco.

title: Ratatouille Niçoise
description: A vibrant Provençal vegetable stew showcasing summer produce. Serve hot or cold for a fresh, flavourful dish.

steps.instruction: Chop all vegetables into even-sized chunks. Set aside.
Heat olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add chopped onion and garlic; sauté for 3 minutes until softened.
Add bell peppers and courgettes; sauté for 5 minutes. Stir in aubergine and cook for another 5 minutes.
Add canned tomatoes, tomato paste, herbes de Provence, salt, and pepper. Stir well, reduce heat to low, and simmer for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Remove from heat. Garnish with fresh basil if using. Serve warm or at room temperature.

title: Croque Monsieur
description: A classic French grilled ham and cheese sandwich, often served with béchamel sauce. Simple yet indulgent comfort food.

steps.instruction: Prepare the béchamel sauce: Melt 20g butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Stir in flour and cook for 1-2 minutes.
Gradually whisk in milk until smooth. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring constantly, until thickened. Season with salt, nutmeg, and pepper. Set aside to cool.
Butter one side of each bread slice. Place 4 slices butter-side down in a baking dish. Top each with ham, 50g cheese, and béchamel sauce.
Top with remaining bread slices, butter-side up. Spread remaining béchamel on top and sprinkle with remaining 50g cheese.
Bake at 200°C (180°C fan) for 15-20 minutes until golden and bubbling. Broil for 2 minutes for extra crispiness if desired.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a light red such as Beaujolais or a dry white like Chablis.
tips: Use good-quality ham and cheese for the best flavour.
For extra crispiness, press the sandwiches gently with a spatula while grilling.

title: Bouillabaisse
description: A traditional Provençal fish stew originating from Marseille. A fragrant, spicy dish perfect for seafood lovers.

steps.instruction: Clean and prepare seafood: debeard mussels, scrub clams, and scale fish. Discard any open shells that don't close when tapped. Set aside.
Heat olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add finely chopped onion, fennel, and garlic; sauté for 5 minutes until softened.
Add tomato, orange zest, saffron, and fennel seeds (if using). Cook for 3 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Pour in white wine and reduce by half. Add fish stock and bring to a simmer. Cook for 10 minutes.
Add mussels and clams, cover, and cook for 5 minutes. Add fish fillets and cook for another 5-7 minutes until seafood is cooked through.
Discard any unopened shells. Ladle into bowls, garnish with fresh parsley, and serve with rouille and crusty bread.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a full-bodied white wine such as a Cassis or a light red like Bandol.
tips: Use the freshest seafood possible for the best flavour.
Make the broth ahead of time for a more flavourful bouillabaisse.

title: Tarte Tatin
description: An upside-down caramelized apple tart that originated in the Loire Valley. A perfect balance of sweet and tart flavours.

steps.instruction: Peel, core, and quarter apples. Arrange them in a 23cm ovenproof frying pan, rounded side down.
Melt 75g butter with 150g sugar in a small saucepan over medium heat. Cook until caramel turns golden brown, about 5-7 minutes.
Carefully pour caramel over apples. Bake at 190°C (170°C fan) for 25 minutes.
Meanwhile, make the pastry: rub 100g butter into 200g flour until resembles breadcrumbs. Add 100g sugar, salt, and vanilla. Bind with egg to form dough.
Roll out dough to 18cm circle. Place over apples, tucking edges in. Bake for another 25-30 minutes until golden.
Remove from oven, let cool for 10 minutes, then carefully invert onto a plate. Serve warm with double cream and icing sugar if desired.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a sweet white wine such as a Sauternes or a sparkling Moscato d'Asti.
tips: Use firm apples that hold their shape, like Bramley or Granny Smith.
Don't rush the caramelising process; it needs time to develop a deep flavour.

title: Quiche Lorraine
description: A classic French tart featuring a creamy egg custard with bacon. Rich, savoury, and perfect for brunch or a light dinner.

steps.instruction: Make the pastry: Rub butter into flour until mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add salt and enough water to bring together. Roll out and line a 23cm tart tin. Chill for 30 minutes.
Preheat oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Line pastry with greaseproof paper, fill with baking beans, and bake for 15 minutes. Remove beans and paper, bake for another 5 minutes.
Meanwhile, cook bacon lardons in a pan over medium heat until crispy. Add chopped onion and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Set aside.
Whisk cream, eggs, salt, pepper, and nutmeg together. Stir in bacon, onion, and half the cheese. Pour into pastry case.
Sprinkle remaining cheese on top. Bake for 30-35 minutes until set and golden. Let cool for 10 minutes before slicing.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a crisp white wine like Sancerre or a light red such as Pinot Noir.

title: Bœuf Bourguignon

steps.instruction: Cut the beef into bite-sized cubes and pat dry. Season with salt and pepper, then dust with flour.

title: Tarte Tatin

title: Croque Monsieur
description: A quintessential French grilled ham and cheese sandwich, elevated with a creamy béchamel sauce. Crispy on the outside, gooey on the inside.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Slice the baguette into 4 pieces and lightly toast until just golden.
Make the béchamel sauce: Melt 20g of butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Whisk in the flour to form a roux and cook for 1 minute.
Gradually whisk in the milk until smooth. Cook for 3-4 minutes until thickened. Season with salt, pepper, and nutmeg.
Assemble the sandwiches: Spread a layer of béchamel on each slice of toasted baguette. Top with ham and Gruyère cheese, then add another slice of bread.
Melt the remaining butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Cook the sandwiches for 2-3 minutes on each side until golden and crispy.
Transfer to the oven and bake for 5 minutes to melt the cheese further. Serve hot with a side of mixed salad.

wine_pairing_notes: A light red wine such as a Pinot Noir or a dry white like a Chablis.

title: Salade Lyonnaise
description: A hearty salad from Lyon featuring frisée lettuce, crispy bacon, poached eggs, and a tangy vinaigrette. A perfect balance of textures and flavors.

steps.instruction: Prepare the vinaigrette: Finely chop the shallot. Whisk together vinegar, mustard, salt, and pepper. Gradually whisk in olive oil until emulsified.

Assemble the salad: Tear frisée lettuce into bite-sized pieces and place in a large bowl. Drizzle with vinaigrette and toss gently.
Top the salad with crispy lardons and poached eggs. Season with extra salt and pepper if needed.

title: Tarte Tatin

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Peel, core, and halve the apples. Arrange them in a circular pattern in a 22cm ovenproof frying pan (handle intact).

description: A rich and creamy savory tart from the Lorraine region, traditionally made with bacon and cheese. A timeless classic for any occasion.

title: Tarte Tatin
description: An upside-down caramelized apple tart that's both rustic and elegant. Perfect for a casual dessert or an impressive finish to a meal.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 190°C. Peel, core, and halve the apples.
In an ovenproof frying pan, melt the butter with the sugar over medium heat until it turns a golden caramel color. Add the apples, rounded-side down, and cook for 10 minutes.
Make the pastry by rubbing the butter into the flour with a pinch of salt until crumbly. Add the egg and vanilla, then knead into a dough. Chill for 20 minutes.
Roll out the pastry and place it over the apples, tucking in the edges. Bake for 25-30 minutes until golden. Let it rest for 5 minutes, then carefully invert onto a plate.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a sweet white wine such as a Sauternes or a Muscat de Beaumes de Venise.
tips: Use firm apples like Braeburn or Granny Smith for best results.
For extra flavor, add a cinnamon stick to the caramel while cooking.
embedding_text: Tarte Tatin An upside-down caramelized apple tart that's both rustic and elegant. Perfect for a casual dessert or an impressive finish to a meal. apples caster sugar butter plain flour butter caster sugar egg vanilla extract salt sweet caramelized French autumn winter

title: Miso-Glazed Aubergine
description: Silky roasted aubergine with a sweet-savory miso glaze. A perfect summer dish that balances rich umami flavors.

steps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan).
Slice the aubergines lengthwise into 1cm thick halves. Score the flesh in a crosshatch pattern and brush with sesame oil. Season with salt.
Roast the aubergines for 20 minutes until tender and golden.
Blend miso paste, mirin, sake, sugar, minced garlic, and grated ginger into a smooth glaze. Heat in a small pan for 3 minutes.
Brush the glaze generously over the roasted aubergines. Return to the oven for 5 minutes to caramelize.
Garnish with sesame seeds and sliced spring onions. Serve warm.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a dry Riesling to cut through the richness.
tips: Use a mix of white and red miso for deeper flavor.
For extra crispiness, shallow-fry the aubergines instead of roasting.
embedding_text: Miso-Glazed Aubergine Silky roasted aubergine with a sweet-savory miso glaze. A perfect summer dish that balances rich umami flavors. aubergine miso paste (white) mirin sake sugar sesame oil garlic ginger sesame seeds spring onions savoury umami sweet Japanese summer

title: Zucchini and Mushroom Gyoza
description: Crispy pan-fried dumplings filled with garden vegetables and mushrooms. A crowd-pleasing vegetarian version of a classic dish.

steps.instruction: Finely chop courgette, mushrooms, cabbage, garlic, spring onions, and coriander. Place in a bowl.
Add soy sauce, sesame oil, grated ginger, and a pinch of salt to the vegetables. Mix well.
Place 1 tsp of filling in the center of each gyoza wrapper. Dampen the edges with water, fold, and pleat to seal.
Heat oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Arrange gyoza seam-side up in the pan. Fry for 2 minutes until golden.
Pour in water, cover, and steam for 5 minutes. Remove lid and fry for another 2 minutes to crisp the bottoms.
Serve hot with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chili oil.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a crisp Sake or a light Pinot Noir.
tips: For extra flavor, add a spoonful of miso to the filling mixture.
Use a non-stick pan to prevent sticking.

title: Cold Soba Noodle Salad with Peanut Dressing
description: Chilled buckwheat noodles tossed in a creamy peanut dressing with fresh vegetables. A refreshing summer dish with an Asian twist.

steps.instruction: Cook soba noodles according to package instructions. Drain, rinse under cold water, and set aside.
Julienne carrot, thinly slice cucumber, dice bell pepper, and slice spring onions. Blanch edamame in boiling water for 2 minutes, then shock in ice water.
Toast peanuts in a dry pan for 3 minutes until fragrant.
Whisk together peanut butter, soy sauce, rice vinegar, sesame oil, honey, lime juice, grated ginger, and 2 tbsp water until smooth.
In a large bowl, toss soba noodles, vegetables, edamame, and half the peanuts with the peanut dressing.
Serve in bowls, garnished with remaining peanuts and a lime wedge.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a chilled Gewürztraminer or a dry sparkling wine.
tips: For a gluten-free option, ensure the soba noodles are 100% buckwheat.
Add sliced avocado for extra creaminess.

title: Teriyaki Tempeh Donburi
description: A hearty bowl of steamed rice topped with crispy teriyaki-glazed tempeh and quick-pickled vegetables. A protein-rich vegetarian take on a classic.

steps.instruction: Cook jasmine rice according to package instructions. Set aside to keep warm.
Slice tempeh into thin rectangles. Heat oil in a pan over medium heat and sauté tempeh for 4 minutes per side until crispy. Set aside.
For the teriyaki glaze, combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a saucepan. Simmer for 5 minutes until thickened.
Slice carrot and daikon into thin matchsticks. Toss with rice vinegar, sugar, and a pinch of salt. Let marinate for 10 minutes.
Brush the teriyaki glaze over the crispy tempeh. Return to the pan for 1 minute to caramelize.
Assemble bowls with rice, topped with tempeh, pickled vegetables, sliced spring onions, and a sprinkle of sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a full-bodied red like Cabernet Sauvignon or a rich Merlot.
tips: For extra crunch, air-fry the tempeh instead of pan-frying.
Add a fried egg on top for a non-vegetarian version.

title: Spicy Miso Ramen with Tofu
description: A fragrant bowl of ramen with a spicy miso broth, chewy noodles, and silken tofu. A comforting dish with layers of umami and heat.

steps.instruction: Slice mushrooms, carrot, and bok choy into thin strips. Mince ginger and garlic.
In a pot, sauté ginger, garlic, mushrooms, and carrot in sesame oil for 4 minutes. Add dashi stock and bring to a simmer.
Stir in miso pastes and soy sauce. Simmer for 10 minutes. Mix cornstarch with 2 tbsp water and add to thicken the broth.
Blanch bok choy in the broth for 1 minute. Remove with a slotted spoon and set aside.
Cook ramen noodles according to package instructions. Drain and divide into bowls.
Ladle hot broth over noodles. Top with bok choy, cubed tofu, spring onions, a drizzle of chili oil, and a squeeze of lime.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a bold Shiraz or a spicy Zinfandel.
tips: For extra richness, add a spoonful of tahini to the broth.
Use fresh ramen noodles for the best texture.

title: Teriyaki Tofu Donburi
description: A comforting bowl of steamed rice topped with crispy teriyaki-glazed tofu and pickled vegetables. Perfect for a quick yet satisfying weeknight meal.

steps.instruction: Press the tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cut into cubes.
Coat the tofu cubes in cornstarch.
Pan-fry the tofu in vegetable oil over medium heat until golden and crispy on all sides.
In a small saucepan, combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. Simmer until the mixture thickens into a glaze.
Pour the glaze over the tofu and toss to coat evenly.
Cook the sushi rice according to package instructions.
Thinly slice the carrot and cucumber, then toss with rice vinegar.
Assemble the donburi by placing rice in bowls, topping with tofu, pickled vegetables, and garnishing with sesame seeds and spring onion.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry Riesling or a light Sake complements the teriyaki glaze.
tips: For extra crispiness, double-fry the tofu.
Use leftover rice for best texture.

Perfect for a quick yet satisfying weeknight meal. firm tofu sushi rice soy sauce mirin sake sugar cornstarch vegetable oil carrot cucumber rice vinegar sesame seeds spring onion savoury sweet Japanese spring autumn

title: Miso-Glazed Salmon with Stir-Fried Vegetables
description: Succulent salmon fillets glazed with a sweet and savory miso sauce, served with a medley of stir-fried vegetables. A nutritious and flavorful meal.

steps.instruction: Whisk together miso paste, mirin, sake, and sugar to make the glaze.
Marinate the salmon fillets in half of the glaze for 10 minutes.
Heat vegetable oil in a pan over medium heat and sear the salmon (skin-side down) for 4 minutes. Flip and cook for another 3 minutes.
Brush the salmon with the remaining glaze and cook for an additional 2 minutes. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, add sesame oil, minced garlic, and ginger. Sauté for 30 seconds.
Add bell pepper, aubergine, shiitake mushrooms, and bok choy. Stir-fry for 6-8 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Drizzle the stir-fried vegetables with the remaining glaze and toss to coat.
Serve the salmon on a bed of stir-fried vegetables, garnished with sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Chardonnay or a dry Gewürztraminer pairs well with the miso glaze.
tips: Do not overcook the salmon; it should be just opaque in the center.
Use a mix of mushrooms like shiitake and oyster for added depth.

title: Okonomiyaki with Cabbage and Pork Belly
description: A savory Japanese pancake packed with cabbage, chunks of pork belly, and topped with tangy okonomiyaki sauce. A hearty and interactive dish.

steps.instruction: Thinly slice the cabbage and chop the pork belly into small chunks.
In a large bowl, mix flour, dashi stock, egg, salt, and pepper to form a batter.
Fold in the cabbage, pork belly, and tenkasu until evenly distributed.
Heat vegetable oil in a large pan over medium heat. Pour in the batter and shape into a round pancake, about 2 cm thick.
Cook for 6-8 minutes until the bottom is golden brown, then flip carefully and cook for another 6-8 minutes.
Transfer the okonomiyaki to a plate, then generously drizzle with okonomiyaki sauce and Japanese mayonnaise.
Sprinkle bonito flakes and aonori over the top, then serve hot.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry Rosé or a light Sake balances the richness of the dish.
tips: For a vegetarian version, replace pork belly with mushrooms or tofu.
Use a spatula to flip the okonomiyaki halfway to ensure even cooking.

title: Chilled Soba Noodles with Sesame Dressing
description: Refreshing buckwheat noodles served cold with a nutty sesame dressing and an array of fresh toppings. A perfect summer dish.

steps.instruction: Cook the soba noodles according to package instructions, rinse under cold water, and drain well.
Thinly slice the cucumbers, carrot, radish, and spring onions. Cut the silken tofu into small cubes.
In a bowl, whisk together sesame paste, soy sauce, rice vinegar, mirin, sugar, and water to make the dressing.
Toss the cooked soba noodles in the dressing until evenly coated.
Arrange the dressed noodles on a plate, top with cucumber, carrot, radish, tofu, and spring onions.
Drizzle with extra dressing if desired, then sprinkle with toasted sesame seeds. Serve with nori sheets on the side.
For extra chill, serve with ice cubes on the side or place the plate over a bed of ice.

wine_pairing_notes: A light and crisp Sake or a dry Pinot Noir.
tips: Use 100% buckwheat soba noodles for a gluten-free option (ensure the brand is certified).
For added protein, include edamame or soft-boiled eggs.

title: Yakitori Chicken Skewers with Shio Koji
description: Juicy chicken skewers marinated in shio koji and grilled to perfection, served with a side of tangy tare sauce. A flavorful and easy appetizer.

steps.instruction: Cut the chicken thigh into bite-sized pieces, then thread onto wooden skewers.
In a bowl, mix shio koji, soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, minced garlic, and vegetable oil to make the marinade.
Coat the chicken skewers in the marinade and let marinate for at least 30 minutes (or overnight for deeper flavor).
Grill the skewers over medium-high heat, turning occasionally, until the chicken is cooked through and slightly charred (about 8-10 minutes).
While grilling, brush the skewers with any remaining marinade for extra flavor.
Slice the spring onion thinly and sprinkle over the cooked skewers.
Garnish with sesame seeds if desired and serve hot with steamed rice.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry Rosé or a crisp Sake complements the smoky flavors.
tips: For extra tenderness, score the chicken pieces before marinating.
If you can't find shio koji, use 1 tbsp salt, 1 tbsp rice vinegar, and 2 tbsp koji (available in Asian markets).

title: Teriyaki Tofu Donburi
description: A comforting bowl of steamed rice topped with crispy pan-fried tofu glazed in a sweet and savoury teriyaki sauce. Quick to prepare and perfect for a weeknight dinner.

steps.instruction: Press the tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cut into 1.5cm cubes.
Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a small saucepan. Simmer on medium heat for 5 minutes to make the teriyaki sauce.
Coat tofu cubes in cornflour, shaking off any excess.
Heat vegetable oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Pan-fry tofu cubes for 3-4 minutes on each side until golden and crispy.
Pour the teriyaki sauce over the tofu and simmer for 2 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly.
Serve hot over steamed rice, garnished with chopped spring onions and sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry Riesling complements the sweet-savoury balance of the dish.
tips: For a gluten-free version, use tamari instead of soy sauce.
Press the tofu thoroughly to achieve maximum crispiness.

Quick to prepare and perfect for a weeknight dinner. rice firm tofu soy sauce mirin sake sugar cornflour vegetable oil spring onion sesame seeds savoury sweet Japanese spring autumn winter

title: Miso-Glazed Aubergine
description: Tender aubergine slices roasted until caramelised and coated in a rich, umami-packed miso glaze. A simple yet sophisticated side dish or light main.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200°C (180°C fan) and line a baking tray with parchment paper.
Slice aubergines into 1.5cm thick rounds, sprinkle with salt, and let sit for 10 minutes to remove bitterness. Pat dry.
Whisk together miso paste, mirin, sugar, soy sauce, sesame oil, and minced garlic to make the glaze.
Brush both sides of aubergine slices with the miso glaze and arrange on the baking tray.
Roast for 20-25 minutes, flipping halfway, until aubergines are tender and caramelised.
Garnish with sesame seeds and chopped spring onions before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: A light, crisp Sauvignon Blanc balances the miso's depth.
tips: Choose firm aubergines with shiny skin for the best texture.
For extra flavour, add a dash of grated ginger to the glaze.

A simple yet sophisticated side dish or light main. aubergine white miso paste mirin sugar soy sauce sesame oil garlic sesame seeds spring onion umami savoury Japanese summer autumn

title: Spicy Tantanmen Noodles
description: A hearty bowl of wheat noodles in a fiery sesame-chilli broth, loaded with minced pork and fresh vegetables. Rich, warming, and deeply satisfying.

steps.instruction: Prep vegetables: thinly slice cabbage, julienne carrot, and finely chop spring onions (separate whites and greens). Mince garlic and ginger.
Heat vegetable oil in a pot over medium heat. Add minced pork and cook until browned, breaking it up with a spoon.
Add garlic, ginger, and sake to the pork. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Stir in tahini, sesame paste, chilli oil, soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and 500ml water. Bring to a simmer.
Add cabbage and carrot to the broth. Simmer for 5 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Cook noodles separately according to package instructions. Drain and add to bowls.
Ladle the spicy broth over the noodles. Garnish with spring onion greens and extra chilli oil if desired.

wine_pairing_notes: A bold red like Zinfandel stands up to the dish's heat and richness.
tips: For extra depth, toast the sesame paste lightly before adding to the broth.
Adjust chilli oil to taste if you prefer milder spice.

title: Okonomiyaki (Savoury Pancake)
description: A thick, pancake-like dish packed with finely shredded cabbage, bacon, and a tangy okonomiyaki sauce, topped with creamy mayo and bonito flakes. A fun, interactive meal to share.

steps.instruction: Shred cabbage finely and place in a large bowl. Add bacon cut into small pieces, flour, egg, dashi stock, and a pinch of salt. Mix well.
Heat vegetable oil in a non-stick frying pan over medium heat. Pour in the batter, shaping it into a round pancake about 2cm thick.
Cook for 4-5 minutes until the bottom is golden, then carefully flip and cook the other side for another 4-5 minutes.
Transfer the pancake to a plate and generously drizzle with okonomiyaki sauce and Japanese mayonnaise in a criss-cross pattern.
Sprinkle bonito flakes and chopped spring onions over the top. They will wobble with the heat from the pancake.
Slice into wedges and serve with pickled ginger on the side.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry Riesling cuts through the richness of the pancake.
tips: Use a non-stick pan to avoid sticking. A metal spatula works best for flipping.
For extra crunch, add a sprinkle of tenkasu (tempura scraps) to the batter.

title: Chawanmushi (Savoury Egg Custard)
description: A delicate, steamed custard made with eggs, dashi, and an assortment of delicate fillings such as shrimp and shiitake mushrooms. Silky smooth and subtly sweet.

steps.instruction: Prepare fillings: peel and devein shrimp, slice shiitake mushrooms, and blanch ginkgo nuts if using.
In a bowl, whisk eggs, dashi stock, mirin, soy sauce, and salt until well combined but not frothy.
Lightly oil four small ceramic cups or ramekins. Divide shrimp, shiitake, and ginkgo nuts evenly among them. Pour the egg mixture over the fillings, filling each cup 80% full.
Place cups in a steamer basket over simmering water. Cover with foil and steam for 12-15 minutes until just set (they should jiggle slightly in the centre).
Garnish each cup with a small sprig of mitsuba and serve warm.

wine_pairing_notes: A light sake or sparkling wine complements the custard's subtle flavours.
tips: Strain the egg mixture for an ultra-smooth texture. Avoid overfilling the cups.
Use fresh, high-quality dashi for the best flavour.

title: Miso-Glazed Aubergine
description: Sweet and savory glazed aubergine is a classic Japanese side dish. It pairs perfectly with steamed rice for a simple, satisfying meal.

steps.instruction: Slice the aubergine into 1cm thick rounds.
Heat a pan with a little oil and sauté the aubergine slices until golden on both sides. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, mix miso paste, mirin, soy sauce, sugar, and water. Simmer for 3 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly.
Return the aubergine slices to the pan, spooning the glaze over them. Cook for another 2 minutes until well-coated.
Drizzle with sesame oil and sprinkle with sesame seeds before serving.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry white wine like Sauvignon Blanc complements the sweetness of the miso glaze.
tips: Use Japanese eggplant for a sweeter, less seedy result.
If gluten-free, substitute tamari for soy sauce.

title: Tempura Vegetable Platter
description: Light, crispy tempura vegetables served with a dipping sauce. A popular Japanese dish enjoyed across all seasons.

steps.instruction: Prepare the dipping sauce by simmering soy sauce, dashi stock, mirin, and salt. Add katsuobushi and let steep for 5 minutes. Strain and set aside.
Slice vegetables into even pieces (thin rounds for courgette, wedges for sweet potato, and lengthwise for mushrooms).
Mix tempura flour with ice-cold water just before coating vegetables. Do not overmix.
Heat oil in a deep pan to 180°C. Dip vegetables in the batter, let excess drip off, and fry until golden (2-3 minutes). Drain on wire rack.

wine_pairing_notes: A light and dry sparkling sake or Japanese beer enhances the crisp texture of the tempura.
tips: Keep the batter ice-cold to ensure a light coating.
Fry in small batches to avoid oil temperature drop.
Substitute dashi with kombu dashi for a vegan option.

title: Tofu and Vegetable Hot Pot
description: A comforting and healthy hot pot featuring tofu and seasonal vegetables in a savory broth. Perfect for cold evenings.

steps.instruction: Prepare dashi stock by simmering with ginger for 10 minutes. Add soy sauce, mirin, and sake; simmer for another 5 minutes.
Slice vegetables into bite-sized pieces: mushrooms, bok choy, carrot into rounds, and spring onions diagonally.
Place tofu, mushrooms, and carrots into the broth and simmer for 5 minutes. Add bok choy and spring onions; cook for another 3 minutes.

wine_pairing_notes: A light and slightly sweet Japanese white wine like Riesling pairs well with the umami flavors.
tips: Use kombu dashi for a fully vegan broth.
Prepare extra broth for a comforting soup later.
Top with shredded nori for added umami.

title: Okonomiyaki (Savory Pancake)
description: A hearty, customizable pancake packed with cabbage, vegetables, and protein. Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki is layered, not mixed.

steps.instruction: Thinly slice cabbage and finely chop spring onions. Slice bacon into small pieces.
Mix flour, water, and egg into a thick batter. Fold in cabbage, spring onions, and tenkasu.
Heat oil in a pan, pour in half the batter, and cook on low heat for 5 minutes. Top with bacon, then add remaining batter. Cook another 5 minutes.
Flip the pancake carefully and cook for another 5 minutes.
Drizzle with okonomiyaki sauce and mayonnaise. Sprinkle soy sauce and katsuobushi on top.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry sake or crisp lager beer balances the richness of the okonomiyaki.
tips: Use a non-stick pan for easy flipping.
For vegetarian, replace bacon with shiitake mushrooms.
Prepare toppings before cooking to assemble quickly.

title: Chilled Soba Noodles with Sesame Dressing
description: Refreshing and light, these chilled soba noodles with a nutty sesame dressing are perfect for hot days. A simple yet delicious summer dish.

steps.instruction: Cook soba noodles according to package instructions in boiling water. Rinse under cold water to stop cooking and cool completely.
Blanch edamame in boiling water for 2 minutes, then rinse in cold water. Thinly slice cucumber and carrot into matchsticks. Slice spring onions diagonally.
Toast sesame seeds in a dry pan for 2 minutes until fragrant. Set aside.
Whisk together tahini, soy sauce, rice vinegar, honey, mirin, and a splash of water to make the dressing.
Toss noodles and vegetables with dressing. Serve chilled, garnished with sesame seeds and ice cubes to keep it cold.

wine_pairing_notes: A crisp and dry white wine like Pinot Grigio or a refreshing beer with citrus notes complements the dish.
tips: Use 100% buckwheat soba noodles for a gluten-free option (check labels).
Add shredded nori or pickled ginger for extra flavor.
Prepare dressing in advance for easier assembly.

title: Teriyaki Tofu Donburi
description: A comforting bowl of steamed rice topped with glossy teriyaki-glazed tofu and quick-pickled vegetables. Perfect for a quick, balanced dinner with minimal effort.

steps.instruction: Press tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cut into 1.5 cm cubes.
Heat oil in a pan and sauté tofu until golden on all sides. Set aside.
In the same pan, combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. Simmer until reduced to a syrupy glaze.
Slice carrot and cucumber thinly, then toss with rice vinegar and a pinch of salt for quick pickles.
Cook rice according to package instructions. Assemble bowls with rice, tofu, and pickles. Drizzle with teriyaki sauce and sprinkle sesame seeds if using.

wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Japanese sake or a dry white wine like Sauvignon Blanc complements the sweet-savory flavors.
tips: Use extra-firm tofu for better texture.
Prepare pickles ahead of time for added convenience.

title: Yakitori-Style Aubergine
description: Grilled aubergine skewers glazed with a tangy soy-based sauce and sprinkled with shichimi togarashi. A smoky, savory dish that’s surprisingly easy to make.

steps.instruction: Cut aubergine into 3 cm chunks and soak in salted water for 10 minutes to remove bitterness.
Thread aubergine chunks onto skewers (soak wooden skewers in water first).
Heat oil in a pan and sauté aubergine until lightly charred on all sides. Set aside.
In a small saucepan, combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and minced garlic. Simmer until thickened.
Coat skewers in the sauce and grill or broil for 2 minutes until sticky. Sprinkle with shichimi togarashi and serve.

wine_pairing_notes: A light-bodied red like Pinot Noir or a chilled lager beer pairs well with the smoky-sweet flavors.
tips: Use metal skewers for easier grilling.
Brushing skewers with extra sauce while grilling enhances flavor.

title: Spinach and Mushroom Gomaae
description: Blanched spinach and shiitake mushrooms tossed in a rich, nutty sesame dressing. A classic Kyoto side dish that’s both healthy and delicious.

steps.instruction: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 30 seconds, then drain and rinse under cold water. Squeeze out excess water and chop roughly.
Slice shiitake mushrooms thinly. Heat oil in a pan and sauté mushrooms until tender.
Toast sesame seeds in a dry pan until fragrant (about 2 minutes). Grind half of them in a mortar for the dressing.
Mix ground sesame seeds, soy sauce, sugar, and water in a bowl. Stir in whole sesame seeds.
Toss spinach and mushrooms in the sesame dressing. Serve chilled or at room temperature.

wine_pairing_notes: A clean, crisp sake or a dry white wine like Pinot Grigio balances the nutty richness.
tips: Use fresh spinach for the best texture.
Soaking shiitake mushrooms in warm water before slicing enhances flavor.

A classic Kyoto side dish that’s both healthy and delicious. baby spinach shiitake mushrooms white sesame seeds soy sauce sugar vegetable oil water nutty umami savoury Japanese spring autumn

title: Okonomiyaki with Courgette and Bacon
description: A savory Japanese pancake packed with shredded courgette, bacon, and cabbage, topped with okonomiyaki sauce and mayo. A hearty meal that’s fun to make and eat.

steps.instruction: Shred cabbage and courgette (no need to peel). Mix with flour, egg, and 120ml water to form a batter. Fold in bacon and tenkasu if using.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Spoon 4 portions of batter into the pan and flatten slightly.
Cook for 5-6 minutes until the bottom is golden and crisp. Flip carefully and cook for another 5 minutes.
Transfer to plates and drizzle with okonomiyaki sauce and mayo. Top with beni shoga if using.

wine_pairing_notes: A fruity red wine like Beaujolais or a crisp beer cuts through the richness.
tips: Use a non-stick pan for easy flipping.
For extra crispiness, press down gently on the pancakes while cooking.

title: Cold Soba Noodles with Sesame Dressing
description: Chilled buckwheat soba noodles served with a creamy sesame dressing, crisp vegetables, and a tangy ponzu dip. A refreshing dish perfect for hot days.

steps.instruction: Cook soba noodles according to package instructions. Drain, rinse under cold water, and set aside.
Thinly slice pak choi, cucumber, and carrot. Blanch pak choi in boiling water for 30 seconds, then cool in ice water.
Toast sesame seeds in a dry pan until fragrant (2 minutes). Blend with soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, tahini, and water until smooth.
Arrange noodles on a plate with vegetables. Drizzle with sesame dressing. Serve with ponzu sauce and wasabi on the side.

wine_pairing_notes: A light, dry sake or a crisp white wine like Riesling enhances the refreshing flavors.
tips: Use 100% buckwheat soba for gluten-free option.
Chill the noodles thoroughly before serving for the best texture.

title: Teriyaki Tofu Donburi
description: A comforting bowl of steamed rice topped with sweet and savory teriyaki-glazed tofu, paired with quick-pickled cucumbers. Perfect for a quick and satisfying meal.

steps.instruction: Press the tofu for 15 minutes to remove excess water, then cut into 1 cm cubes.
Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a bowl to make the teriyaki sauce.
Heat oil in a pan, add tofu, and sauté until golden brown on all sides.
Pour the teriyaki sauce over the tofu and simmer until the sauce thickens and coats the tofu.
Slice the cucumber thinly and toss with rice vinegar. Cook the rice according to package instructions.
Assemble bowls with rice, top with teriyaki tofu, and garnish with pickled cucumbers and sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry sake or a crisp white wine like Sauvignon Blanc complements the sweet-savory flavors.
tips: Use extra-firm tofu for better texture.
For a gluten-free version, substitute soy sauce with tamari.

title: Soba Noodle Salad with Edamame
description: Chilled soba noodles tossed with crisp vegetables, edamame, and a tangy sesame dressing. A refreshing and light dish ideal for summer.

steps.instruction: Cook soba noodles according to package instructions, rinse under cold water, and drain.

steps.name: Blanch edamame in boiling water for 3 minutes, then drain and cool.
steps.instruction: Continue preparing the dish as described.
Julienne the carrot and thinly slice the red bell pepper.
Finely chop coriander.
Whisk together soy sauce, sesame oil, rice vinegar, honey, and minced garlic to make the dressing.
Toss noodles, edamame, carrot, bell pepper, and coriander with the dressing.
Serve garnished with sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: A light and crisp white wine such as Pinot Grigio pairs well with the sesame and tangy flavors.
tips: Use 100% buckwheat soba noodles for a gluten-free option.
For extra protein, add grilled tofu or tempeh.

title: Japanese Curry Rice with Sweet Potato
description: A hearty and mildly spiced Japanese curry with tender sweet potatoes, carrots, and onions, served over steamed rice. A family-friendly dish that’s easy to make.

steps.instruction: Peel and cut sweet potato and carrots into 2 cm chunks. Thinly slice onion.
Heat oil in a pot, sauté onion until translucent, then add garlic and curry roux. Stir for 2 minutes.
Add sweet potato, carrot, water/stock, soy sauce, and honey. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 15 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Cook rice according to package instructions.
Serve hot curry over steamed rice.

wine_pairing_notes: A full-bodied red wine like Merlot or a dry Riesling can cut through the richness of the curry.
tips: For extra depth, add a bay leaf or a piece of kombu while simmering.
Use apple or honey for a touch of sweetness to balance the spices.

title: Okonomiyaki (Savory Pancake)
description: A crispy, savory pancake packed with shredded cabbage, batter, and your choice of toppings like pork belly or shrimp. Topped with okonomiyaki sauce and mayo.

steps.instruction: Shred cabbage and thinly slice spring onion. If using pork belly, slice into bite-sized pieces.
In a bowl, mix flour, dashi stock, and egg to make a batter. Fold in cabbage, tenkasu, and spring onion.
Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Spread the batter into a round pancake (about 2 cm thick).
If using pork belly, place slices on top of the pancake. Cover and cook for 8-10 minutes until the bottom is crispy and golden.
Carefully flip the pancake and cook for another 5-7 minutes.
Drizzle okonomiyaki sauce and mayonnaise over the top. Sprinkle with bonito flakes and aonori (if using). Serve hot.

wine_pairing_notes: A dry and crisp white wine such as Sake or a light beer complements the rich flavors.
tips: Use a well-heated pan to ensure a crispy bottom.
Customize with shrimp or squid instead of pork for a pescatarian version.

title: Miso-Glazed Aubergine with Sesame Seeds
description: Silky roasted aubergine slices smothered in a rich miso glaze, garnished with sesame seeds. A simple yet flavorful vegetarian dish inspired by Kyoto-style cuisine.

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 200°C. Slice aubergine into 1.5 cm thick rounds.
Brush aubergine slices with vegetable oil, season with salt, and roast for 20 minutes until tender.
In a bowl, whisk together miso paste, sugar, mirin, sake, and sesame oil to make the glaze.
Spread a thin layer of glaze over each aubergine slice and return to the oven for 5 minutes.
Garnish with sesame seeds and thinly sliced spring onion. Serve warm or at room temperature.

wine_pairing_notes: A smooth and slightly sweet white wine like a Riesling balances the umami richness of the miso.
tips: For extra flavor, add a dash of yuzu juice to the glaze.
Use Japanese eggplant for a sweeter and less bitter taste.

title: Miso-Glazed Aubergine (Nasu Dengaku)
description: Sweet and savory miso glaze transforms simple aubergine into a rich, umami-packed dish. Traditionally served as a side, it pairs beautifully with rice.

steps.instruction: Slice the aubergine in half lengthwise and score the flesh in a crosshatch pattern. Salt lightly and let sit for 10 minutes.
Rinse off the salt and pat dry. Brush with oil and bake at 200°C for 15 minutes until tender.
Mix miso, mirin, sake, and sugar in a bowl. Spread over baked aubergine halves and broil for 5 minutes until glazed.
Garnish with sesame seeds and serve warm.

wine_pairing_notes: Dry sake or a crisp white wine like Sauvignon Blanc.
tips: Use Japanese white miso for authentic flavor.
Brushing with extra mirin before serving enhances the glaze.

title: Vegetable Tempura Donburi
description: Light, crispy tempura vegetables served over steamed rice in a savory dashi broth. A comforting and visually appealing dish.

steps.instruction: Prepare the batter by whisking flour, cornflour, and ice-cold water until just combined. Keep batter cold.
Slice vegetables into even pieces. Heat oil to 180°C.
Dip vegetables in batter, let excess drip off, and fry in batches until golden and crispy (2-3 minutes). Drain on wire rack.
Cook rice according to package instructions.
Simmer dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar for 5 minutes to make the broth.
Assemble bowls with rice, top with tempura vegetables, and pour hot broth over the top.

wine_pairing_notes: Dry Japanese rice wine (sake) or a light lager.
tips: Keep the batter cold to prevent it from absorbing too much oil.
Use a candy thermometer to maintain oil temperature for even frying.

title: Spinach and Tofu Gomaae
description: A simple yet flavorful dish of blanched spinach tossed in a nutty sesame dressing with silken tofu. Quick and healthy.

steps.instruction: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 30 seconds, then plunge into ice water. Squeeze out excess water and chop roughly.
Drain tofu and pat dry. Crumble into bite-sized pieces.
Blend sesame paste, soy sauce, sugar, rice vinegar, and water into a smooth dressing.
Toss spinach, tofu, and dressing together. Garnish with sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: Light green tea or a crisp white wine like Pinot Grigio.
tips: Use a food processor to quickly blend the sesame dressing.
Chill the dish for 10 minutes before serving to let flavors meld.

title: Teriyaki Mushroom and Leek Donburi
description: Succulent mushrooms and leeks glazed in a glossy teriyaki sauce over steamed rice. A hearty vegetarian alternative to classic teriyaki.

steps.instruction: Slice mushrooms and leeks into even pieces.
Cook rice according to package instructions.
Heat oil in a pan and sauté mushrooms and leeks for 5 minutes until softened.
Add soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar to the pan. Simmer for 3 minutes.
Mix cornflour with water to make a slurry, then stir into the pan to thicken the sauce (1 minute).
Serve hot over rice.

wine_pairing_notes: Dry rosé or a light-bodied red wine like Beaujolais.
tips: Use a mix of mushroom types for depth of flavor.
Add a dash of garlic for extra aroma.

title: Soba Noodles with Cold Sesame Dressing (Zarunashi Soba)
description: Chilled buckwheat soba noodles tossed in a creamy sesame dressing with fresh herbs and cucumber. Refreshing and light.

steps.instruction: Cook soba noodles according to package instructions, then rinse under cold water to cool. Drain well.
Cut cucumber into thin strips. Finely chop spring onion and coriander.
Blend sesame paste, soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and water into a smooth dressing.
Toss noodles with dressing, cucumber, spring onion, and coriander. Garnish with sesame seeds.
Serve chilled with ice cubes on the side (optional).

wine_pairing_notes: Dry white wine like Albariño or a light beer.
tips: Use 100% buckwheat soba for gluten-free option.
Soak sesame paste in water for 5 minutes before blending for a smoother texture.

title: Teriyaki Tofu Donburi
description: A hearty and savory bowl of steamed rice topped with crispy teriyaki-glazed tofu and fresh summer vegetables. Perfect for a quick weeknight dinner.

steps.instruction: Press the tofu between paper towels to remove excess water, then cut into 1.5 cm cubes.
In a bowl, mix soy sauce, sake, mirin, and sugar to make the teriyaki sauce.
Heat oil in a pan over medium heat and sauté tofu cubes until golden and crispy on all sides, about 8 minutes.
Add the teriyaki sauce to the pan and simmer for 2 minutes until the tofu is glazed.
Steam the rice according to package instructions.
Slice aubergine and courgette into thin rounds and sauté in a separate pan with a little oil for 3 minutes until tender. Set aside.
Assemble bowls by placing steamed rice at the bottom, topping with glazed tofu, sautéed vegetables, and sliced spring onions. Sprinkle with sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: Pair with a dry Riesling to complement the sweet-savoury teriyaki.
tips: For extra crispiness, coat the tofu cubes in cornstarch before frying.
Use day-old rice for better texture in the donburi.
Substitute sake with mirin if avoiding alcohol.

title: Thai Green Curry with Aubergine
description: A fragrant and creamy green curry made with fresh green curry paste, coconut milk, and tender aubergine. Serve with steamed jasmine rice for a comforting meal.

steps.instruction: Cook jasmine rice according to package instructions.
In a pot, heat oil and fry green curry paste for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add coconut milk and bring to a gentle simmer. Stir in fish sauce and palm sugar.
Add sliced aubergine and baby corn. Cook for 10 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Turn off heat, stir in Thai basil leaves, and squeeze lime juice. Serve hot with rice.

wine_pairing_notes: A crisp German Riesling balances the heat and creaminess.
tips: Use fresh green curry paste for the best flavor.
Adjust fish sauce to taste for a vegetarian alternative, use soy sauce.
Add more coconut milk if a thinner curry is desired.

title: Som Tum (Green Papaya Salad)
description: A refreshing and spicy salad made from shredded green papaya, tomatoes, green beans, and a tangy lime dressing. Perfect for hot summer days.

steps.instruction: Shred green papaya, carrot, and green beans into thin strips.
In a mortar, pound garlic, chilies, and peanuts into a rough paste.
Add shredded vegetables, tomatoes, and dressing ingredients. Toss well.
Adjust seasoning with more lime juice, fish sauce, or sugar if needed.
Garnish with extra peanuts and serve immediately.

wine_pairing_notes: A light and fruity Pinot Noir complements the spicy and tangy flavors.
tips: Use unripe papaya for the authentic texture.
For a milder salad, remove seeds from chilies before pounding.
Add dried shrimp for a non-vegetarian version.

title: Pad Kra Pao (Thai Basil Stir-Fry)
description: A quick and aromatic stir-fry of minced meat with Thai basil and chili. Serve with steamed rice and a fried egg on top for a hearty meal.

steps.instruction: Cook jasmine rice according to package instructions.
In a bowl, mix fish sauce, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and palm sugar. Set aside.
Heat oil in a wok, stir-fry minced garlic and chilies for 1 minute.
Add minced chicken and cook until browned. Drain excess fat.
Pour sauce mixture and stir well. Add basil leaves and toss. Serve with rice and a fried egg.

wine_pairing_notes: A refreshing lager beer cuts through the heat and richness.
tips: Use fresh Thai basil for the best flavor.
For a vegetarian version, replace chicken with firm tofu.
Adjust chili quantity to suit spice preference.

title: Massaman Curry with Potato and Beef
description: A rich and mildly spicy curry slow-cooked with tender beef, potatoes, and peanuts in a fragrant coconut milk sauce. A comforting dish for family gatherings.

steps.instruction: Cut beef into bite-sized pieces and potatoes into chunks.
In a pot, fry curry paste in oil for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add coconut milk, cinnamon, star anise, fish sauce, and palm sugar. Bring to a simmer.
Add beef and potatoes. Simmer for 45 minutes until beef is tender.
Stir in peanuts and adjust seasoning. Serve with jasmine rice.

wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Syrah pairs well with the rich and spiced flavors.
tips: Use a heavy pot for even cooking.
For a vegetarian version, replace beef with mushrooms or jackfruit.
Add more cinnamon and star anise for a deeper flavor.

title: Mango Sticky Rice
description: A classic Thai dessert with sweet coconut sticky rice served with fresh ripe mango slices. Simple yet irresistibly delicious.

steps.instruction: Soak glutinous rice in water for at least 4 hours or overnight.
Steam soaked rice for 20 minutes until tender.
Mix coconut milk, palm sugar, and salt. Heat gently until sugar dissolves.
Pour half the coconut mixture over steamed rice. Let it absorb for 10 minutes.
Slice mangoes and serve with rice. Drizzle remaining coconut mixture and sprinkle sesame seeds.

wine_pairing_notes: A sweet Moscato d'Asti complements the tropical flavors.
tips: Use the ripest mangoes for the best flavor.
For extra flavor, add a pinch of pandan leaves while cooking rice.
Can be served chilled for a refreshing treat.
